snippet: However, narrow beams also make the first networking step, neighbor discovery (ND), substantially harder.
context: Directional millimeter-wave and sub-terahertz links are attractive for UAV swarms because narrow beams can compensate for severe path loss and reduce interference. However, narrow beams also make the first networking step, neighbor discovery (ND), substantially harder. In a three-dimensional UAV-UAV network, two nodes can establish a link only when their transmit and receive beams satisfy a bidirectional angular alignment condition.

snippet: Deterministic methods design scan schedules to improve coverage and sometimes provide worst-case delay guarantees, but their performance often depends on schedule synchronization, network scale, topology assumptions, or beam-codebook structure.
context: Existing directional ND protocols can be broadly categorized as deterministic and random methods. Deterministic methods design scan schedules to improve coverage and sometimes provide worst-case delay guarantees, but their performance often depends on schedule synchronization, network scale, topology assumptions, or beam-codebook structure. Random methods require less coordination and adapt naturally to distributed deployments, but the resulting discovery process can have a long tail: some important links remain undiscovered for many slots, slowing the formation of a connected discovered-neighbor graph.

snippet: Oblivious ND later established a theoretical foundation for distributed directional discovery without prior coordination or synchronization \cite{Chen2017ObliviousND}.
context: Early directional ND work studied probabilistic transmission and listening strategies for nodes with directional antennas \cite{Vasudevan2005DirectionalND}. Oblivious ND later established a theoretical foundation for distributed directional discovery without prior coordination or synchronization \cite{Chen2017ObliviousND}. Hunting-based directional ND and FastND further studied blind mmWave spatial rendezvous and accelerated 60-GHz directional discovery, but mainly as scan/beam-learning problems under communication-side assumptions \cite{Wang2017HDND,Zhou2018FastND}.

snippet: The learning contribution is deliberately scoped: the current evidence supports structured MARL for ISAC-assisted candidate reduction and large-scale contention control, not an optimal MAC theorem.
context: This work uses a shared-parameter actor-critic MARL environment to train decentralized mode/beam policies under centralized training and decentralized execution. The learning contribution is deliberately scoped: the current evidence supports structured MARL for ISAC-assisted candidate reduction and large-scale contention control, not an optimal MAC theorem. The resulting policy is tested for zero-shot transfer across swarm sizes while preserving local-observation execution.

snippet: When $\Rs=\Rc$ appears in the final single-hop transfer setting, it should be read only as a matched-support operating point; system-level ISAC range behavior can change with carrier frequency, aperture, power, waveform, target scattering, and detection threshold, so the supplementary range sweep varies $\Rs/\Rc$ rather than treating equality as a hardware law \cite{Bomfin2024SystemISAC}.
context: We therefore do not set $\Rs=\Rc$ by default and do not derive $\Rs$ from a specific radar equation or estimator threshold; instead, $\Rs$, false-alarm probability, missed-detection probability, angular-cell error, and sensing staleness are protocol-level input parameters that emulate the service-quality point delivered by an underlying ISAC receiver. When $\Rs=\Rc$ appears in the final single-hop transfer setting, it should be read only as a matched-support operating point; system-level ISAC range behavior can change with carrier frequency, aperture, power, waveform, target scattering, and detection threshold, so the supplementary range sweep varies $\Rs/\Rc$ rather than treating equality as a hardware law \cite{Bomfin2024SystemISAC}. The observation does not reveal neighbor identity, position, velocity, or range-resolved neighbor state.

snippet: The discovered graph is a finite-horizon neighbor-knowledge graph, or discovered-edge cache, and should not be confused with a proof that the instantaneous active communication graph is connected under arbitrary later mobility.
context: Within a finite discovery horizon $T$, the protocol aims to build a useful discovered graph rather than to maximize only the number of random handshakes. The discovered graph is a finite-horizon neighbor-knowledge graph, or discovered-edge cache, and should not be confused with a proof that the instantaneous active communication graph is connected under arbitrary later mobility. In the main single-hop tests, $\Rc$ and the region size are chosen so that communication-range eligibility is persistent during the discovery episode; mobility-boundary tests are reported separately.

snippet: We use $\lambda_2$ as a topology-quality proxy related to consensus performance in networked agents \cite{OlfatiSaber2004Consensus}, but this paper does not prove a closed-loop consensus-control convergence theorem.
context: It is a local rule designed to encourage finite-time formation of a well-connected discovered graph. We use $\lambda_2$ as a topology-quality proxy related to consensus performance in networked agents \cite{OlfatiSaber2004Consensus}, but this paper does not prove a closed-loop consensus-control convergence theorem.

snippet: First, per-beam collision memory records repeatedly risky beam cells.
context: The contention-aware actor used in the strongest MARL evidence line adds four problem-specific structures. First, per-beam collision memory records repeatedly risky beam cells. Second, a local contention-state vector summarizes recent success, failure, collision, empty-beam feedback, candidate sparsity, and topology deficit.

snippet: The SkyOrbs-like baseline implements a deterministic three-dimensional skip-scan schedule inspired by SkyOrbs, but it is not a strict reproduction of the complete SkyOrbs protocol.
context: The comparison set includes uniform random scanning, a SkyOrbs-like directional baseline, communication-only MAPPO, communication-only contention MAPPO, the ISAC contention actor, and four ISAC gate profiles. The SkyOrbs-like baseline implements a deterministic three-dimensional skip-scan schedule inspired by SkyOrbs, but it is not a strict reproduction of the complete SkyOrbs protocol. The no-ISAC learning baselines disable sensing-derived occupancy priors, candidate masks, and beam-lock updates at execution.

snippet: The adaptive, topology-preserving, and balanced-topology gates are reported as operating-point ablations rather than as universally dominant replacements.
context: The default gated actor is selected as the main low-collision balanced profile. The adaptive, topology-preserving, and balanced-topology gates are reported as operating-point ablations rather than as universally dominant replacements. Mechanism ablations remove candidate-set refinement, beam-lock refinement, or topology-deficit scoring from the proposed protocol.

snippet: We also report collision-penalized discovery in supplementary dense-regime analyses and assumed radio-state accounting as diagnostic evidence; these metrics are not presented as a platform-calibrated energy-optimality result.
context: Because high-percentile discovery delays can be censored by a finite simulation horizon, the main claims emphasize discovery rate, empty-scan ratio, edge count, and connectivity metrics. We also report collision-penalized discovery in supplementary dense-regime analyses and assumed radio-state accounting as diagnostic evidence; these metrics are not presented as a platform-calibrated energy-optimality result. For range sweeps, $D$ denotes the region diagonal.

snippet: The curve is empirical training-diagnostic evidence, not a proof of convergence.
context: The policy is trained only in the small source setting with $N=10$, 10-degree beams, and 300 slots per episode; the plotted horizontal axis is the cumulative environment step. The curve is empirical training-diagnostic evidence, not a proof of convergence. The supplementary material reports per-episode return, discovery, $\lambda_2$, collision, policy-loss, value-loss, entropy, and memory curves from the same 100-episode, 30000-step training trace.

snippet: Thus the supported claim is a tunable cross-layer operating frontier, not a single gate that dominates every metric.
context: The topology-preserving and balanced-topology variants fill out the ablation frontier, showing that stronger topology preservation usually restores raw discovery and $\lambda_2$ at the cost of additional collisions. Thus the supported claim is a tunable cross-layer operating frontier, not a single gate that dominates every metric. The supplementary learned-component ablation further separates trained weights from structured priors: random or zero-weight policies can increase raw discovery through aggressive access, whereas the trained actor reduces collisions by 81.4\%--85.2\% in the focused probe, supporting a collision-efficiency claim rather than a universal raw-discovery dominance claim.

snippet: In the $N=100$, 10-degree ablation, candidate-set refinement causes the largest observed performance loss when removed, suggesting that ISAC-assisted candidate formation is the dominant mechanism for the main operating point rather than a universal claim across all beamwidths and mobility models.
context: In the $N=100$, 10-degree ablation, candidate-set refinement causes the largest observed performance loss when removed, suggesting that ISAC-assisted candidate formation is the dominant mechanism for the main operating point rather than a universal claim across all beamwidths and mobility models. At $N=100$ and 10-degree beams, removing the ISAC-driven candidate-set refinement reduces the discovery rate from 0.3655 to 0.0313 and drives the discovered graph connectivity to nearly zero.

snippet: This B=10 result should not be read as collision optimality: the supplementary paired B=15 checks show that one-slot delay can be more collision-efficient, while a ten-seed local role-control probe can mitigate that boundary, making collision-aware MAC refinement a separate protocol-layer problem.
context: This supports interpreting the proposed protocol as a low-latency design point and the delayed variant as a practical implementation boundary. This B=10 result should not be read as collision optimality: the supplementary paired B=15 checks show that one-slot delay can be more collision-efficient, while a ten-seed local role-control probe can mitigate that boundary, making collision-aware MAC refinement a separate protocol-layer problem. Fig.~\ref{fig:ablation_discovery} gives the corresponding ablation comparison.

snippet: First, the learning evidence is a shared-parameter actor-critic MARL line trained in the $N=10$, 10-degree, 300-slot source setting and transferred to $N=100$ tests; it should be interpreted as structured MARL evidence for ISAC-assisted contention control, not as an exhaustive comparison against all MAPPO, QMIX, HAPPO, MAT, transformer-based, or graph-neural MARL variants.
context: This study has several important limitations. First, the learning evidence is a shared-parameter actor-critic MARL line trained in the $N=10$, 10-degree, 300-slot source setting and transferred to $N=100$ tests; it should be interpreted as structured MARL evidence for ISAC-assisted contention control, not as an exhaustive comparison against all MAPPO, QMIX, HAPPO, MAT, transformer-based, or graph-neural MARL variants. Second, the SkyOrbs-like comparison is an inspired baseline rather than a strict reproduction of the complete original algorithm.

snippet: Second, the SkyOrbs-like comparison is an inspired baseline rather than a strict reproduction of the complete original algorithm.
context: First, the learning evidence is a shared-parameter actor-critic MARL line trained in the $N=10$, 10-degree, 300-slot source setting and transferred to $N=100$ tests; it should be interpreted as structured MARL evidence for ISAC-assisted contention control, not as an exhaustive comparison against all MAPPO, QMIX, HAPPO, MAT, transformer-based, or graph-neural MARL variants. Second, the SkyOrbs-like comparison is an inspired baseline rather than a strict reproduction of the complete original algorithm. Third, ISAC is represented by an abstract beam-cell occupancy prior; physical waveform design and estimator implementation are outside the scope.

snippet: Fourth, candidate-set refinement abstracts sub-slot or readiness behavior; the one-slot delayed candidate-set ablation bounds this implementation assumption but does not replace a hardware-specific timing study.
context: Third, ISAC is represented by an abstract beam-cell occupancy prior; physical waveform design and estimator implementation are outside the scope. Fourth, candidate-set refinement abstracts sub-slot or readiness behavior; the one-slot delayed candidate-set ablation bounds this implementation assumption but does not replace a hardware-specific timing study. Fifth, extremely narrow beams at 3 and 5 degrees, the archived 30-degree boundary sweep, and abrupt mobility models remain stress or boundary regions and should be discussed as such.

snippet: The gate-family results mitigate this boundary by exposing low-collision, balanced, and topology-heavy operating points, but a complete collision- and energy-aware MAC remains outside the present scope.
context: Therefore, collision-penalized discovery, raw discovery rate, and $\lambda_2$ should be interpreted together in dense finite-horizon settings. The gate-family results mitigate this boundary by exposing low-collision, balanced, and topology-heavy operating points, but a complete collision- and energy-aware MAC remains outside the present scope.

snippet: The additional figures and tables below are used to bound the claims rather than to assert universal robustness.
context: The main paper should be read as a cross-layer link-layer neighbor-discovery study: ISAC provides an imperfect beam-cell occupancy prior, and neighbor edges are created only by bidirectional narrow-beam handshakes. The additional figures and tables below are used to bound the claims rather than to assert universal robustness.

snippet: It should not be interpreted as a strict reproduction of the complete SkyOrbs protocol: the original beam-indexing order, scan-cycle synchronization, antenna switching, motion assumptions, and published-trend reproduction have not all been reconstructed here.
context: It uses the same beam codebook, mobility trace, range setting, slot horizon, and evaluation metrics as the proposed protocol, but disables ISAC-derived occupancy, candidate, and beam-lock observations. It should not be interpreted as a strict reproduction of the complete SkyOrbs protocol: the original beam-indexing order, scan-cycle synchronization, antenna switching, motion assumptions, and published-trend reproduction have not all been reconstructed here. Accordingly, the main claim is not a head-to-head claim against complete SkyOrbs; the SkyOrbs-like results are used to contextualize deterministic communication-only scan scheduling, while the core evidence comes from no-ISAC controls and ISAC mechanism ablations.

The broader sweep separately covers $\Rc/D\in\{0.65,0.85,1.05\}$ and $\Rs/\Rc\in\{0.5,0.75,1.0,1.25\}$ so that the protocol claim does not depend on assuming equal communication and sensing supports.

snippet: That behavior is not the same as a better protocol: their collision counts increase by factors of 5.4 and 6.7 relative to the trained full actor.
context: Table~\ref{tab:supp_learned_ablation} and Fig.~\ref{fig:supp_learned_ablation} show that random-weight and zero-weight/rule-only policies can produce higher raw discovery and $\lambda_2$ because they access the channel aggressively. That behavior is not the same as a better protocol: their collision counts increase by factors of 5.4 and 6.7 relative to the trained full actor. The trained actor reduces mean collisions by 81.4\% relative to random weights and by 85.2\% relative to the zero-weight/rule-only control, yielding a higher collision-penalized discovery rate than both controls.

snippet: The flat stochastic actor reaches a mean discovery rate of 0.632, while the full candidate-score/topology/rule-residual actor reaches 0.557.
context: In a N=10, B=72, 80-slot behavior-cloning probe with three training seeds and five held-out evaluation episodes per seed, stochastic evaluation is nonzero for all evaluated variants. The flat stochastic actor reaches a mean discovery rate of 0.632, while the full candidate-score/topology/rule-residual actor reaches 0.557. Thus the structured neural probe does not yet outperform the flat stochastic student.

snippet: However, the full structured actor improves deterministic execution from 0.0015 discovery for the flat actor to 0.0643 and makes 14 out of 15 deterministic evaluations nonzero.
context: Thus the structured neural probe does not yet outperform the flat stochastic student. However, the full structured actor improves deterministic execution from 0.0015 discovery for the flat actor to 0.0643 and makes 14 out of 15 deterministic evaluations nonzero. It also reduces stochastic empty-scan ratio from 0.690 for the flat actor to 0.111, at the cost of unresolved collision coordination.

snippet: A short 10-episode actor-critic fine-tuning follow-up increases the full structured stochastic discovery rate to 0.586 and leaves deterministic discovery near 0.066, but it still does not overtake the flat stochastic student.
context: This supports using the structured actor as a feasible neural extension, not as the current main method. A short 10-episode actor-critic fine-tuning follow-up increases the full structured stochastic discovery rate to 0.586 and leaves deterministic discovery near 0.066, but it still does not overtake the flat stochastic student. A residual-strength sweep identifies $\lambda_{\mathrm{rule}}=0.25$ as a better balanced setting in this probe, with stochastic discovery 0.598 and deterministic discovery 0.084 with all 15 deterministic evaluations nonzero.
